channel.title: caffeineletter
channel.description: From choosing your coffee to how minute modifications change your taste notes - learn it all here.

item.title: Brew Water - The silent variable
item.description: Coffee is 98% water. Let's look at how it can change the cup.
item.dc:creator: Aravind Balla
item.content:encoded:
Last month I travelled with my own coffee. Same beans, the same grinder in my bag, the same recipe I have brewed a hundred times at home.

The cup came out wrong. Flat, a little hollow, none of the sweetness I was expecting. I blamed the grind, the kettle, the unfamiliar kitchen.

It was none of those. It was the water.

I only connected the dots reading the second chapter of The Physics of Filter Coffee. Your cup is about 98% water. It was never a neutral backdrop, it is an ingredient.

First, two things called TDS

This trips people up, so let me clear it early.

When we talked about ratios, TDS meant the strength of your cup. How much dissolved coffee sits in the final drink.

Today's TDS is a different thing. It is the total dissolved minerals in your water, measured in parts per million (ppm), before the coffee ever touches it. That is what we are talking about here.

What is the ideal level

The SCA points to around 150 ppm, with a workable range of 75-250 ppm and a near-neutral pH.

Go below ~75 ppm and there are not enough minerals to pull flavour out. The cup turns flat and hollow. This is the issue in most of our Indian homes, where RO purifiers strip water down to 10-30 ppm.

Go above ~250 ppm and things get muddy and chalky, and your kettle starts collecting scale.

Here is roughly where common packaged water sits:

  • Aquafina (PepsiCo): 10-50 ppm

  • Kinley (Coca-Cola): 50-100 ppm

  • Bisleri: 70-150 ppm

  • Himalayan (Tata): 150-300+ ppm

Bisleri lands right in the sweet spot. That is why it is my no-fuss pick for brewing at home. It is also the water they handed me at the AeroPress championship last year, which tells you something.

It is not just the number

Is all water at 150 ppm the same?

TDS is a blunt total. Two waters can both read 150 ppm and taste completely different, because what matters is what is dissolved in them.

Two players to know.

Hardness is your calcium and magnesium. This is the extraction muscle. Magnesium is especially good at grabbing flavour compounds, while calcium leans more towards body.

Buffer, or alkalinity, is mostly bicarbonate. It neutralises acids. Too much and your bright, fruity acids get flattened into a dull cup. Too little and the coffee turns sharp and sour. The SCA target sits around 40 ppm.

Measuring it at home

A pocket TDS meter is cheap, around ₹300-500 on Amazon. It gives you the total number in seconds, though not the calcium-versus-bicarbonate split.

If you want to go further, people sell brew water serums and sachets that build water up to a specific recipe. Worth knowing they exist, even if you never go down that road.

Do you know the TDS for the water you brew with, {{ first_name | friend }}? Or has it been the silent variable in your cup all along?

More Reading

  • Aeropress Championship 2025 Lessons

  • Sour vs Bitter notes: How to identify them

That’s all I have. Have a caffeinated weekend.

See you.

Keep on brewing!


Powered by beehiiv

item.title: The Neo Brew
item.description: Let us break down a recent pour over recipe from the Japanese coffee legend, Tetsu Kasuya.
item.dc:creator: Aravind Balla
item.content:encoded:
A coffee video surfaced on my YouTube feed last week. Tetsu Kasuya, the man behind the 4:6 method, had dropped a brand new recipe. Ten years after he won the World Brewers Cup, he is back with something he is calling THE NEO BREW.

I watched it. Then I went and brewed it.

A few things about it sound completely wrong on paper. And yet the cup comes out sweet, with a heavy, smooth body.

Going against the trend

Most pour over advice lately pulls in one direction. Two or three small, gentle pours. Short contact time, somewhere around 2 to 3 minutes. The whole idea is to suppress the bad stuff, mainly bitterness, and chase a clean, tea like cup.

Tetsu calls this deductive brewing. You are trying to lose as few points as possible to harshness.

His new recipe plays the opposite game. It is additive. He stretches the brew out to around three and a half minutes across ten pours, and instead of hiding the negatives, he goes chasing the positives. The sweetness, the body.

Screenshotted and translated from the above video.

The recipe

Here is what you need.

  • Coffee: 20g, light roast recommended

  • Water: 300g, so a 1:15 ratio

  • Temperature: 95-96°C

  • Grind: extra coarse, around 40-45 clicks on a Comandante C40. If you use a different grinder, our grind size converter shows you how many clicks you need on yours

  • Dripper: Hario NEO, but any cone bottom one like a V60 works fine

The pouring is the interesting part. You pour all 300g in ten pours of 30g each, spaced 15 seconds apart. Pour each one in a quick, even stream and don't let the water pool and sit on the bed. The whole brew runs long, landing around three and a half minutes.

One small tip: Go with easily divisible weight of coffee. Let me explain. If you take 12g of coffee, total water weight becomes 180g which is 18g each pour. Where would stop on the 6th pour? If you can't answer that, 15g or 20g of coffee is your option.

Why it works

Tetsu breaks it into four ideas that feed each other.

The extra coarse grind means fewer fines and less surface area. So even with hot water sitting on it for a while, you are not dragging out the late, harsh, bitter compounds. We talked about those extraction stages here.

The high temperature then does the heavy lifting, making up for the coarse grind and pulling components thoroughly out of those big chunks.

The ten pours are the clever bit. A coarse grind on its own would under-extract and leave you with a thin, watery cup. Pouring small and often gets around that, dissolving a bit more into the water with each fresh pass while keeping the bed hot.

And the long brew time, that full three and a half minutes, is what builds the thick mouthfeel. The drawn-out contact keeps pulling the rich, heavy compounds that give the cup its body.

Put together at 1:15, you land on the bullseye Tetsu is aiming for. Sweet, full bodied, and still clean.

Screenshotted and translated from the above video.

My take

The best part is you don't need a fancy grinder for this.

We are grinding coarse, and grinders are far more consistent at coarse settings than fine ones. The boulders and stray fines that wreck a fine grind matter much less here. So that cheap hand grinder will do just fine.

It pairs best with a fruity light roast. Keep a timer running for those 15 second gaps. And if your cup tastes thin, go a touch hotter or one notch finer before changing anything else.

I have been a small pour person for a while now, so this one flipped things for me.

Which camp are you in, {{ first name | friend }}? And will you give Tetsu's new one a go next?

That’s all I have. Have a caffeinated weekend.

See you.

Keep on brewing!


Powered by beehiiv

item.title: Aeropress Soup
item.description: Two filters, a gentle press, zero pressure. The soup method might be the cleanest cup your AeroPress makes.
item.dc:creator: Aravind Balla
item.content:encoded:
A few mornings ago I made a cup with my AeroPress that stopped me mid-sip. Clean, bright, properly sweet. This is the kind of clarity I usually only chase with a careful pour over, but rounder and more concentrated.

I'd been putting off trying the soup method for weeks. One cup in and I'm a convert. Over the next few days, I tried this method with all the coffees on my shelf to see how they taste.

What on earth is "soup"?

The name sounds like it's about the thick look of the brew. It isn't. SOUP stands for Spro, Only Un-Pressurized. Spro is just barista slang for espresso.

It started with people tinkering with low pressure shots on the Oxo brewer, and a few folks trying the same trick on the AeroPress on YouTube. Then Lance Hedrick put out a video on it and the whole thing took off. Now r/AeroPress and r/espresso are full of people making soup.

The promise being the clarity and sweetness of filter coffee, with a lot more punch. And no 9 bar machine in sight.

Why it tastes so clean

Quick refresher. In a real espresso, water is forced through a tamped puck at 9 bars of pressure. That pressure builds body and crema, but it's also what causes channeling, where water blasts through the weak spots in the puck. Channeling is where a lot of espresso's harshness and astringency come from.

Soup throws the pressure out. You press gently, under 1 bar, almost a slow drip. No pressure, no channeling, no astringency. What's left is sweetness.

It also leans on percolation. Fresh water moving through the bed extracts cleaner than coffee just sitting in water, something I rambled about in the Hario Switch post. The double paper filter handles the rest, catching the oils and fines that would otherwise muddy things up. (More on why paper gives a cleaner cup in the filters post.)

How I make it

You would need an Aeropress and two filter papers.

  • Grind like you do for a pour over.

  • 20g coffee, 200g water at 96°C.

  • Put Aeropress in the default position, the first filter paper in the cap and fit it to the body.

  • Coffee grounds in and tamp the bed flat with the plunger, using the second paper.

  • Take the plunger out after tamping. This would also get the second filter paper along with it. So we put it back in and place it down with a spoon or something. This paper would make the top of the bed to properly sandwich the coffee with 2 filter papers.

  • Pour, on the center of the filter paper, gently. Let it stay for about 10 seconds, then plunge slow and gentle. A fast, low pressure flow is the goal, not an arm workout.

It's a touch finicky. Getting the top filter to sit flat takes a couple of goes.

And yes, the purists will say no pressure means it isn't really espresso. They're right. It isn't. But I'm not chasing espresso. I'm chasing a clean, sweet cup, and soup delivers. It shines brightest with light roasts and a grinder that gives you an even grind.

This is worth an experiment if you own an Aeropress.

So {{ first_name | friend }}, are you going to give soup a shot before splurging on an espresso machine? And which beans would you reach for first?

That’s all I have. Have a caffeinated weekend.

See you.

Keep on brewing!


Powered by beehiiv

item.title: Extraction, Fast and Slow
item.description: We talk about extraction like it's one thing. It isn't. It's two processes, diffusion and advection, quietly working together. Understanding them makes every dial you turn finally make sense.
item.dc:creator: Aravind Balla
item.content:encoded:
I've been reading The Physics of Filter Coffee by Jonathan Gagné. He's an astrophysicist, so the book is exactly as nerdy as you'd expect. I love it.

One idea in the first chapter completely reframed how I think about brewing. We always talk about extraction like it's one thing happening in the cup. It's not. It's actually two separate processes working together. Once you see them, you can't unsee.

This is a continuation of our extraction chats. Expect more science from this book in the coming posts.

The two parts

When hot water hits a coffee ground, getting flavour into your cup happens in two steps.

Diffusion moves the dissolved compounds from deep inside the ground out to its surface.

Advection is the flowing water sweeping those compounds off the surface and into your cup.

From the book, The Physics of Filter Coffee

Diffusion, the slow one

Inside a coffee particle, the solubles are packed tight at high concentration. Nature doesn't like that. Those compounds slowly migrate towards areas of lower concentration, which is the water sitting around the ground.

Picture a drop of ink in a glass of still water. It spreads on its own, no stirring needed. Just slowly.

That's diffusion. The bigger the gap between "lots of compounds inside" and "clean water outside," the faster it happens. If the surrounding water gets crowded with dissolved coffee, that gap shrinks and diffusion grinds to a halt.

Advection, the fast one

This is where the water does the heavy lifting.

The moment a compound reaches the surface, moving water grabs it and carries it off, fast. But the clever part is what this does for diffusion. By constantly clearing away the dissolved coffee, advection keeps the water around the ground hungry. The concentration gap stays wide, so diffusion never stalls.

The two feed each other.

After writing about these two, I knew exactly what to name this post.

Why percolation beats immersion at extraction

This is where it clicks for me. Remember the two brewing mechanisms?

In percolation (your V60, your pour over), fresh water flows through the bed the whole time. Strong advection, gap stays wide, diffusion runs at full tilt. Efficient and clean. I said this back in the Hario Switch post without fully knowing why. Now I do.

In immersion (French press), the water just sits there. Advection is weak. As solubles build up, the gap flattens and extraction slowly self-limits. That's exactly why immersion is forgiving but caps out. It also explains why a good stir helps. You're faking advection.

Grind size and the journey

Grind size is really about distance.

In a coarse grind, a compound at the core has a long way to travel before it reaches the surface. The boulders barely get touched, so they under-extract. Go finer and that journey shrinks, plus you expose more surface area.

This is also why different compounds come out at different times. The small, soluble acids escape early and easily. The heavier bitter compounds need a finer grind and more time to make it out. We saw the taste side of this in the sour vs bitter post.

Once you see it this way, every dial you touch makes sense. Grind, temperature, agitation, pour speed. You're only ever nudging one of two things, diffusion or advection.

So {{ first_name | friend }}, does thinking about your brew this way change anything for you? Hit reply and tell me.

That’s all I have. Have a caffeinated weekend.

See you.

Keep on brewing!


Powered by beehiiv

item.title: Low Acidity Coffee for the Indian Stomachs
item.description: A few simple habits to drink gentler, with Indian beans that happen to be perfect for it.
item.dc:creator: Aravind Balla
item.content:encoded:
There was a phase where my morning coffee started biting back.

Black filter coffee, first thing, on an empty stomach. By 10am my gut felt like it was holding a grudge. I touched on this in the yerba mate post - the churn you get when coffee's acidity acts up.

My friends keep asking this - Is there a way to drink coffee without the acidity wrecking my stomach?

Turns out, the answer is more interesting than "buy dark roast beans".

The acidity isn't really the problem

Here is the thing that surprised me.

Coffee sits around a pH of 4.9 to 5.1. That makes it less acidic than orange juice, and nowhere close to a cola. So the actual acid in your cup is fairly mild.

The stomach trouble comes from elsewhere. Caffeine relaxes the valve between your stomach and food pipe (the LES), which lets acid creep upward. And coffee, even decaf, nudges your stomach into making more acid of its own. The chlorogenic acid in the beans adds to the irritation.

So low acidity coffee is a bit of a misnomer. We are really chasing a cup that is gentler on the gut. A few habits stacked together do most of the heavy lifting.

Quick note - I brew coffee, I am not a doctor. If your stomach trouble is frequent or severe, please go see one.

Stop drinking it empty

This is the cheapest fix and needs no new beans.

Coffee on an empty stomach hits the hardest. Have it with food, even a couple of biscuits or a small breakfast alongside. This single change sorted out most of my morning churn and gut health in general.

Reach for gentler beans

Acidity is tied to where coffee grows. High altitude beans develop more of it, low altitude ones less.

This is where Indian coffee shines. Monsooned Malabar is the obvious pick. Beans aged in monsoon winds for weeks, left with near zero acidity, a heavy earthy body, and that musty character. Wayanad robusta works too, since robusta carries far less chlorogenic acid than arabica. We went deep on these in the origin and varietal post.

Internationally, Brazilian and Indonesian beans lean the same way.

And if caffeine is your real trigger, decaf is worth a look.

Go darker, or go cold

Roasting breaks down chlorogenic acids, so darker roasts are lower in acid and rounder in the cup. There is a bonus here too. Dark roasting develops a compound called NMP that may actually tell your stomach to ease off on acid production, which is why some people handle dark roast better. I covered brewing these in the dark roasts post.

Cold brew is the other big win. That long, cold steep pulls out far less acid than hot water does. Even though I don't like this so much, sometimes I run a batch at 1:8 and it is noticeably gentler on the stomach. My method is in the cold brew tonic post.

Milk buffers some of the acid as well. Sadly, lactose and I are not friends, so I skip that one.

One more thing to note - matcha and yerba mate are less acidic in general. So when your stomach acts up, switching to these for a week and get your gut back to normal.

None of these are magic on their own. But food first, gentler beans, and a darker or colder brew, stacked together, let me keep my daily cup without paying for it later.

So {{ first_name | friend }}, does coffee ever upset your stomach? What is your trick for it? Hit reply, I am collecting these.

That’s all I have. Have a caffeinated weekend.

See you.

Keep on brewing!


Powered by beehiiv

item.title: Do you have a favourite cup?
item.description: Let's talk about how the cup you drink from plays a surprising role in how you perceive your coffee's taste
item.dc:creator: Aravind Balla
item.content:encoded:
How is it going, {{ first name | coffee lover }}?

If you are a cup hoarder like my co-founder, Vikra, you might have a favourite cup for a certain type of coffee.

I have a favorite cup as well. The coffee from my favourite cups just feels wonderful.

I was curious why a coffee cup has such a tremendous impact on how we feel about the coffee.

Not long after, I found myself in another rabbit hole. There is science that suggests that the vessel you drink from plays a surprising role in how you perceive your coffee's taste.

Twitter tweet

Why does coffee taste different in different cups?

Eating or drinking is not just how your tongue interprets it, but a complete sensory experience. Which means it's not just our taste buds but also our smell, sight, touch, and even our subconscious expectations that affect the experience.

The smell makes up for more than half of the experience.

We feel we can't taste food when we catch a cold. Wondered why? That's because our smell is blocked. You can test this for yourself by closing your nose and eating something.

This is why the same cups are used when cupping coffee. It’s a standard protocol for grading coffee worldwide.

What does the research say

I brewed some coffee and poured it into 2 different cups. I was fascinated to observe that a heavy black ceramic cup made the coffee feel bold and robust. While another was a light, transparent cup that tasted brighter. However, as I kept sipping, both became almost the same, maybe because I knew the coffees were the same and my brain calibrated to that.

Here are the key differences summarized from my research notes.

Color: White cups tend to amplify the perceived intensity of coffee's flavor compared to transparent mugs. This might be due to the contrast between the dark coffee and the bright white interior, which primes our brain to expect a more robust taste. Blue and clear cups, on the other hand, often lead to the perception of coffee as smoother and less intense.

Shape: The cup’s shape influences more than just how we hold it. Taller, narrower cups tend to concentrate aromatics, enhancing our perception of floral and fruity notes. Wider cups, with their larger surface area, can emphasize chocolate and nutty undertones by allowing more oxygen to interact with the coffee.

Touch: The texture and weight of your cup add another layer to the experience. Heavy cups often make us perceive coffee as richer and more substantial, while lighter cups might lead us to focus more on the brighter, acidic notes.

What you can explore

If you are looking to expand your already large collection of coffee cups, I highly recommend the Aroma series from Origami. I bought an "Aroma" cup from the series a few months ago.

PS: This isn’t an endorsement (we wish it was haha, if you’re a coffee brand, hit us up!)

https://brewmethods.com.au/blogs/product-feature/origami-flavour-cups

Here is some more info about those cups.

https://origami-kai.com/en/products/origami-pinotflavorcup

More coffee talks from us:

  • Better Americano-style drinks

  • How much does a grinder matter for homebrewing

  • Blending different coffee beans

That’s all I have. Have a caffeinated weekend.

See you.

Keep brewing!


Powered by beehiiv

item.title: Cupping Session at Home
item.description: Right after my SCA certification, I cupped coffee at home with friends. Here's how to set up your own.
item.dc:creator: Aravind Balla
item.content:encoded:
As soon as I got back from my SCA certification, I wanted to put it to use. So I called over a few friends, grabbed a few coffees from the same estate but roasted by different roasters, and we ran triangulation tests on my coffee table.

Three cups per round. Two of the same coffee, one different. Pick the odd one out.

SCA cupping is a standardized method developed by the Specialty Coffee Association for the sensory evaluation of coffee. It involves a systematic process to assess the quality of coffee based on its aroma, flavor, acidity, and other characteristics.

What I didn't expect was the conversation. We weren't just guessing, we were describing. Whose roast brought out more sweetness. Whose felt cleaner. Who liked which, and why. By the end, each of us had a much clearer sense of our preferences.

You can do this in your kitchen this weekend.

Why cup at home

A cupping is the cleanest read you can get on a coffee. No grinder dial to second-guess. No pour pattern to blame. Just coffee, water, and you.

It's the same protocol roasters use to evaluate beans, and the principles work just as well on a kitchen counter as in a lab.

What you need

The random numbers on the cup is how you name samples while cupping.

  • 3 matching cups or small bowls, around 200ml each. Mason jars, tea cups, small mixing bowls, anything that matches. The vessel helps because you don't want it changing how the coffee feels in your mouth

  • A scale, a kettle, and a regular tablespoon

  • A timer

The SCA protocol is 8.25g of coffee per 150g of water per cup. I round to 8g and 150g at home. Grind medium coarse, a touch coarser than your pour over. On a Timemore C2, around 26 clicks. Water at 93°C.

Grind coffee for each cup separately. First fill all 3 cups with coffee, take the water at 93°C and just dump it in the cup, no pouring technique needed.

For triangulation, which one of the cupping methods, pick two coffees. Fill two cups with one, one cup with the other. Have someone else arrange them so you don't know which is which.

The SCA form, simplified

The full form has ten attributes. For home, five are enough:

  • Fragrance/Aroma - We measure the intensity of the smell. When the beans are just ground it's the Fragrance (previously called dry aroma), then again after breaking the crust it is the Aroma.

  • Acidity - brightness, not sourness

  • Sweetness - how much natural sweetness comes through

  • Body - light like green tea or heavy like full cream milk

  • Aftertaste - what lingers, and is it pleasant

Score each out of 10. Don't sweat the numbers. The actual effort is isolating each attribute one at a time.

Breaking the crust

At the 4-minute mark, the grounds form a crust on top.

Take your spoon, push the crust back with three gentle strokes, and put your nose right over the cup as you do it. This is the strongest aroma moment of the entire session. All those volatile compounds trapped under the crust come rushing out.

Then skim the floating grounds off the top. Wait a few minutes for the coffee to drop to drinking temperature.

The slurp

Once we hit the 7 minute mark, cup will be around 70°C. Dip your spoon, bring it to your lips, and slurp loudly.

Slurping aerates the coffee and it sprays the liquid across your tongue at once, hitting every taste zone.

Why this trains your palate faster than drinking

Three reasons:

  • Variables are stripped away. It's just the beans. You don't care about grind size, pouring technique, brewer etc.

  • You taste at multiple temperatures. Hot, warm, cool. Different flavours surface at each ones.

  • You're scoring, not just sipping. Putting a number on acidity forces you to actually find it.

One month of weekly home cuppings moves your palate further than a year of just drinking pour overs. It's also the fastest way to stop confusing sour with bitter.

So {{ first_name | friend }}, when are you planning your first cupping? Reply and let me know.

That’s all I have. Have a caffeinated weekend.

See you.

Keep on brewing!


Powered by beehiiv

item.title: Playing with ratio for pourovers
item.description: Turns out ratio is not just controlling the strength, but also the extraction. Once you understand the difference, ratio stops being a fixed setting and becomes a lever you can pull on the same bag of beans. Here's how to play with it.
item.dc:creator: Aravind Balla
item.content:encoded:
One day, my morning cup tasted sour. Sharp, lemony, the kind of "off" that makes you a little disappointed.

Instinct said grind finer. But I tried something else first. I added more water. Same beans, same grind, just bumped the ratio from 1:15 to 1:17.

The cup that came out was sweet and balanced.

That's when it clicked. Ratio isn't just for dialing strength. It's doing two jobs at once.

Strength is not the same as extraction

Two terms worth separating in your head.

Strength (TDS) is how concentrated your cup is. If your coffee measures 1.3% TDS, it means 1.3% of the liquid is dissolved coffee material and the rest is water. Less water, higher concentration, stronger cup.

Extraction is how much of the actual ground coffee you dissolved into the cup. The specialty target sits between 18-22% of the bean's mass.

Water isn't just diluting your coffee, it's the solvent. More water means more opportunity to pull compounds out. So a 1:17 brew isn't just weaker than a 1:15. It's also more extracted.

SCA Brewing Control Chart

We talked about the stages of extraction in this post - acids, then sweetness, then bitters. Ratio decides how far down that line you get to travel.

Going tighter - 1:13 to 1:15

These ratios give you a heavier body and richer mouthfeel. The cup feels intense, concentrated, almost syrup-like.

The risk is under-extraction. With less water doing the work, if your grind isn't dialed in, the early acids come out but the sweet sugars that balance them never get pulled. The cup tastes sour, sometimes salty, sometimes just hollow.

This range works best for medium-dark and dark roasts. Those beans are already broken down from the roast and dissolve easily. A tighter ratio gives you that chocolatey body without dragging out the harsh, ashy compounds that show up late in the brew. We covered this in detail in the dark roasts post.

Going wider - 1:16 to 1:18

These give you a lighter, more tea-like cup. Flavours separate cleanly. You taste notes individually instead of one big mouthful.

The risk flips. With that much water moving through the bed, a fine grind or long contact time tips you into over-extraction. That dry, astringent feeling like over-steeped green tea.

This range shines with dense light roasts - high-altitude washed Ethiopians, complex Centrals. Those beans are stubborn and hard to extract. The extra solvent helps coax out the elusive floral and fruity notes.

Most pros sit here. James Hoffmann's V60 method defaults to 1:16.6. Scott Rao recommends 1:16-1:17 for light roasts. Lance Hendrik almost always suggests 1:17.

A quick cheat sheet

  • Start at 1:16 for any new bean. Universal sweet spot to figure out how it behaves.

  • Tastes sour or sharp? Try more water (1:16.5 or 1:17) before grinding finer. Counter-intuitive, but it works.

  • Tastes bitter or dusty? Try less water (1:15). The bitter compounds get left behind in the filter.

  • Match to roast. 1:14 to 1:15 for medium-darks and darks. 1:16 to 1:17 for lights.

Once you stop treating ratio as a fixed setting and start treating it as a lever, you have a whole new variable to play with on the same bag of beans.

So {{ first_name | friend }}, what ratio do you default to? Have you ever pushed it to either extreme on the same bean and seen how different the cup gets?

That’s all I have. Have a caffeinated weekend.

See you.

Keep on brewing!


Powered by beehiiv

item.title: Better Americano styled drinks
item.description: Most cafes (and we home brewers) make Americanos the lazy way. Let's look at how to pull a proper one at home without the espresso machine, and even an iced drink better than an Iced Americano.
item.dc:creator: Aravind Balla
item.content:encoded:
I was at a cafe in Hyderabad couple of weeks ago and ordered an americano. Then I asked the barista to please give me the espresso shot and hot water in two separate cups.

He looked at me. "But that is americano only."

I said yes, but I want to mix it myself. He shrugged and made it that way.

I'm not just being difficult. It's actually a better way to drink an americano, and once you read why, you might start ordering it the same way. Let's also get into how to make a proper one at home, because most of us homebrewers don't own an espresso machine but can absolutely pull this off.

A quick word on origins

The americano comes from World War II. American soldiers stationed in Italy found espresso too intense and asked baristas to dilute it with hot water. Caffè Americano literally means "American coffee".

I wrote about the full backstory, and how it differs from a long black, in the Americano and Long Black post. Worth a read if you haven't already.

Making one at home with a mokapot

If you don't have an espresso machine, your mokapot is your best friend here. Around 1000 INR, and it gets you close enough.

Brew your mokapot the way you usually do. Then dilute with just off boil water. The ratio I like is 1:2 or 1:3 mokapot coffee to water. Start with 1:2 if you want it strong and adjust from there.

One small thing to keep in mind. Mokapot brews at around 2 bars of pressure compared to espresso's 9. So the base is already a touch less concentrated than a real espresso. Go a little lighter on the dilution than what a cafe would do.

Use a medium to dark roast. Light roasts get too thin and sour once you add water.

Skip the crema for a cleaner cup

Back to my cafe order.

When the barista pours hot water over an espresso shot, the crema breaks and disperses into the drink. Most places will tell you this is fine. But here's the thing. Crema is mostly bitter compounds and oils. Once it disperses in 200ml of water, the bitterness becomes the loudest note. The aromatic complexity, the sweetness, the body, all of it gets diluted.

What you're left with is a thin, mildly bitter cup.

So I ask for the espresso and hot water in separate cups, spoon off the crema layer, and then combine. You get a sweeter, cleaner americano.

This trick mostly applies at cafes. Mokapot pseudo-crema is just air bubbles and dissipates on its own.

If you actually want crema preserved, you don't want an americano. You want a long black.

The aerocano for summer

Iced americanos are not really my preference. Hot espresso meets ice, the ice melts in seconds, and you're left with watery flat coffee. And I find it's just more bitter than a hot americano for some reason.

The aerocano fixes this. It's a chilled, foamed americano that went viral on Reddit in 2022. Texture closer to a nitro cold brew than an iced coffee, no milk involved.

The trick is aeration. Espresso, cold water and ice get steamed (or frothed) to create a thick microfoam on top. The foam gives mouthfeel without milk, locks in the aroma, and the drink doesn't dilute as fast.

Without an espresso machine, here is how I make it:

Brew a strong mokapot shot. Pour it into a milk frothing pitcher with around 60ml cold water and 100g of ice. Aerate with a handheld milk frother for 10 to 15 seconds. Pour into a tall glass over fresh ice.

A french press works too. Plunge fast and aggressively for about 10 seconds. Won't be steam wand perfect, but the texture gets surprisingly close.

So {{ first_name | friend }}, which one are you making first? The separate pour cafe order, the mokapot americano at home, or the aerocano for these summer afternoons?

PS:

Every week, we get dozens of replies with how much you love reading our editions. We read all your emails and are grateful that you’ve taken the time to message us.

There's so much love for Caffeineletter, and we'd want to frame your words on our wall of love.

It'd mean the world if you could share what you feel about Caffeineletter.

That’s all I have. Have a caffeinated weekend.

See you.

Keep brewing!


Powered by beehiiv

item.title: How much does a grinder matter for home brewing?
item.description: Most home brewers obsess over recipes and beans, but skip past the grinder. Let's look at why grind consistency is the hidden variable in every brew, and how an upgrade path might look like.
item.dc:creator: Aravind Balla
item.content:encoded:
I am traveling this week and been making coffee for a lot of people at the place that I stay. Most of them are surprised that I carry a hand grinder even when I travel. I was explaining them how hard it is to go back to pre-ground coffee once you taste the freshly ground ones.

That moment made it click for me, again, how much work the grinder is doing in the background.

Why your grinder matters more than your kettle

The hidden variable in every brew is particle size consistency.

A good grinder gives you uniform particles. A bad one gives you a mix of fines and boulders in the same brew. The fines over-extract and clog your filter. The boulders under-extract because the water just runs past them. You end up with a cup that tastes both bitter and weak. Yes, that's a real thing.

Most stalled brews come back to this. We talked about it in What to do when your brew clogs and Brewing Consistent Pour Overs. Both posts circle back to the same culprit. Grind quality.

Hand vs Electric

I started with a hand grinder, and I still recommend one to most home brewers starting out.

Here's why. In the ₹3,000–6,000 range, hand grinders pack better burrs than electric grinders at the same price. You're paying for the burrs, not the motor.

My own progression went like this. I brewed with the Timemore C2 for years. It was my daily driver, my travel grinder (it still is), my everything. Recently, I moved to the Turin DF54 for the electric convenience and a real jump in burr quality. The difference in cup clarity was immediate.

Hand grinders have their tradeoffs. Arm workout, especially if you're brewing for 2+ people. Slower in general. But mildly meditative if you're in the right mood.

Electric grinders give you speed, hands-free operation, and better consistency across multiple cups. You just pay more for the same burr quality.

If you're switching grinders, your "20 clicks" on one grinder doesn't translate to 20 clicks on another. We built a grind size converter to solve exactly this. Just select your current grinder name and the grind size, then select your new grinder. The converter gives you the equivalent for your new grinder.

Espresso changes the game

Filter brewing is forgiving. A click off and you can fix things with temperature or ratio.

Espresso is not.

We covered this in detail in Science of an Espresso, but the short version is this. You're working with 200–400 micron particles, where halving the size doubles the surface area exposed to water. Most filter grinders either can't go that fine, or their step sizes are too coarse for the precision espresso needs.

This is why "all-rounder" grinders compromise. They either nail filter and feel coarse for espresso, or vice versa.

If you're only brewing pour over and aeropress, any decent burr grinder works. The moment you want to pull espresso shots at home, the budget jumps.

What to look for and where to start

Look for burr quality and consistency over brand name. Adjustment range that matches your brewing methods. Stepless or fine-stepped adjustment if you like to dial things in.

The path I'd suggest. Start with the Timemore C2 or 1Zpresso Q Air. Both are solid daily drivers that will serve you well for years. When you're ready to level up, the Commandante C40 or 1Zpresso J-Ultra are the next tier. Both will likely be the last hand grinder you ever buy.

A good grinder pays itself back over years. It's the one upgrade I'd never go back from.

So {{ first_name | friend }}, what grinder are you using right now? Eyeing an upgrade?

Also, Vikra (both of us run Caffeineletter) and I will be in the mountains for a couple of weeks. We plan to get active on social media, so please follow Caffeineletter on Instagram and Twitter.

That’s all I have. Have a caffeinated weekend.

See you.

Keep on brewing!


Powered by beehiiv

item.title: Benefits of Black Coffee
item.description: A look at what black coffee does for your heart, brain and liver, and why the humble paper filter in your V60 might be the most underrated health tool in your kitchen.
item.dc:creator: Aravind Balla
item.content:encoded:
By this point, we all know coffee is good for us. Not much when it comes to milk based ones, but definitely black coffee. I wanted to actually research and tell why it’s good, not just passing statements.

Coffee is healthy. Especially if you drink it the way most home brewers do, black and through a paper filter.

Let’s look at what studies actually show, and why your morning pour over might be one of the healthier habits in your life.

What the research says

Heart health and longevity. A 2025 Tufts study that tracked 46,000 adults found that 1-2 cups of caffeinated coffee a day was linked to a 16% lower risk of all-cause mortality. 2-3 cups bumped it up to 17%. This benefit showed up strongest among people who drink their coffee black. The moment sugar and saturated fat were added, the numbers started sliding.

Lower risk of Type 2 diabetes. A massive review of 28 studies covering over a million people found a clear dose-dependent response. More coffee, lower risk, all the way up to 6 cups a day. Interestingly, this held true for decaf too, which tells us the benefits aren’t just about caffeine. Chlorogenic acids and other compounds seem to play a big role. We talked about decaf in an earlier post if you want the deep dive.

Brain protection. Multiple studies have linked regular coffee intake to a reduced risk of Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s. A 2012 paper even showed caffeine helps control involuntary movements in people already living with Parkinson's.

Liver health. Coffee drinkers consistently show lower rates of cirrhosis and liver cancer. One large study found four or more cups a day could cut alcoholic cirrhosis risk by up to 80%.

These benefits come from coffee’s bioactive compounds, chlorogenic acids, polyphenols, and caffeine. There’s no single miracle ingredient. It's the whole package.

Why are these benefits only in Black coffee?

Most of the benefits above only show up clearly when the coffee is unadulterated.

A 240ml Starbucks Caramel Macchiato has around 120 calories, 15g of sugar, and 4g of fat. The same amount of your home pour over has ~2 calories and no sugar. These are effectively different drinks.

Before you panic, a splash of milk in your cortado or cappuccino isn’t the villain. It’s the daily habit of loading coffee with sweeteners and syrups that shifts the math.

What’s the role of filter paper in this equation?

Coffee contains two compounds called cafestol and kahweol, both diterpenes that live in the oily fraction of the bean. They’re natural, they’re in every cup, but they raise LDL cholesterol. How much of them end up in your bloodstream depends entirely on how you brew.

A 2025 study from Uppsala University put some numbers on this. Here’s what they found across brewing methods:

  • Paper-filtered coffee: ~12 mg/L cafestol

  • French press, moka, percolator: ~90 mg/L

  • Boiled coffee: ~900 mg/L

  • Espresso: highly variable, some samples reached over 2000 mg/L

The paper fibres physically trap the oils in which these diterpenes live. Metal mesh filters and espresso puck baskets let them pass straight through. A separate Tromsø Study of 21,000+ people found that 3-5 cups of espresso per day was associated with significantly higher serum cholesterol levels.

So your Hario V60 and those Cafec papers? They’re quietly doing cardiovascular work every morning.

This doesn't mean espresso is bad. Moderate espresso intake still shows up in the longevity and metabolic studies. But if you're drinking 3-4 cups daily, a paper filter is the clear winner for cholesterol.

The caveats

None of this means “drink more coffee”. Past 4 cups, the equation tips, you start running into sleep trouble, anxiety, and that jittery edge. We covered some of this in the How much is too much coffee post.

I honestly can’t do more than 2 cups a day.

Your daily pour over ritual is arguably one of the better habits you've built.

So {{ first_name | friend }}, do you drink it black, or do you add something? And has any of this changed how you think about your ritual? Let me know.

That’s all I have. Have a caffeinated weekend.

See you.

Keep on brewing!


Powered by beehiiv

item.title: Coffee Cocktails for the Summer
item.description: Summer is here so we have four boozy iced coffee recipes that put the coffee first.
item.dc:creator: Aravind Balla
item.content:encoded:
Summer is already here. And it's brutal.

A few of you have been asking me about cold coffee drinks that involve alcohol.

If you don't drink alcohol, this post might not be for you. But don't worry, I will have a post on non-alcoholic iced coffee drinks coming soon. You will enjoy that one.

Now, we talked about coffee liqueur a while back. That was fun, but today is simpler. No liqueur, no cream. Just good coffee, a spirit, some ice and maybe a mixer. Coffee first, always.

Your Coffee Base

This matters more than the alcohol.

Cold brew is the easiest summer option. It's already cold. No temperature shock when it hits ice. You can batch it and keep a stash in the fridge. We covered the recipe before. 1:8 ratio, steep for 18-24 hours, strain. Done.

Espresso or mokapot coffee works when you want a sharper, more cocktail-like drink. It stands up better to bold spirits like rum and brandy. But it's hot, so it melts ice faster and dilutes the drink quicker.

A simple way to think about it is cold brew for long sippable drinks and espresso for punchy short ones.

I am not including the Iced Pour over we discussed last time because I like that as is. It comes out too good to experiment. So I didn't experiment much with that yet.

Four Drinks Worth Making

1. Cold Brew + Whiskey

The simplest one. 90ml cold brew, 30ml whiskey, ice. That's it. This is the one I reach for on weekday evenings when I don't want to think. The whiskey's warmth and the cold brew's smoothness just work together. No shaking, no muddling, no effort.

You can also swap the whiskey with rum, I liked it with Old Monk, reminds me of my college rum-and-coke days.

2. Coffee Mojito

60ml cold brew, 30ml white rum, a few mint leaves, a teaspoon of sugar, ice, and top with soda water. Muddle the mint and sugar first before adding anything else. This drinks more like a summer cooler than a coffee cocktail.

3. Espresso Daiquiri

Daiquiri is a classic cocktail with rum, citrus and sweetner. I am a fan of this. Pairing this with espresso, or more like a risterreto which is less bitter, goes really well. It involves some effort but totally worth it.

35ml white rum, 15ml lime juice, 25ml vanilla syrup, and 25ml fresh espresso or strong mokapot coffee. Shake hard with ice and strain into a glass. This is the fanciest one on the list but worth the effort. The lime and vanilla lift the coffee in a way I didn't expect the first time I made it.

4. Orange Coffee Tonic + Gin

We talked about espresso tonic before. This is the summer upgrade. Cold brew or espresso, tonic water, a splash of fresh orange juice, and 30ml gin or vodka over ice. Bright, citrusy, fizzy. This one is perfect for a late morning or brunch.

When to Drink What

Mornings or brunch, go with the orange coffee tonic. With or without the gin. It's light and doesn't feel heavy.

Evenings, cold brew with whiskey or the coffee mojito. Both are easy and need no special equipment.

When you want to impress someone, the espresso daiquiri. It looks and tastes like something from a cocktail bar.

One rule I follow is to keep the spirit around 30ml. Coffee is the main ingredient. The alcohol is just the supporting act, like milk in a cortado.

So {{ first_name | friend }}, which one are you trying first? And if you have a boozy coffee recipe that you love, I want to hear it.


Powered by beehiiv

item.title: Naturally occouring Decaf Coffee
item.description: Let's look at what makes this species of coffee different, the science behind it, and why it matters.
item.dc:creator: Aravind Balla
item.content:encoded:
I was going down a rabbit hole the other night, reading about rare coffee species, and I came across something that genuinely stopped my surfing.

A coffee plant that produces zero caffeine. Not reduced caffeine. Not decaffeinated after harvest. Zero. Naturally.

It's called Coffea charrieriana, or Charrier coffee. And it might change how we think about decaf forever.

What is Coffea charrieriana

We know Arabica. We know Robusta. We even talked about Excelsa. But here's the thing, there are around 120 species under the Coffea genus. Most of them never make it to your cup.

Coffea charrieriana is one such species. It was first collected in 1985 from Cameroon's Bakossi Forest Reserve but wasn't officially described until 2008. Scientists named it after André Charrier, a French geneticist who spent his career studying coffee genetics.

It's a small shrub, barely 1-1.5 metres tall, growing on rocky slopes of wet rainforest at low altitudes around 160-300 metres. To put that in perspective, most specialty Arabica grows between 1,200-2,000 metres. This one stands way below.

The sad part is, it's critically endangered. Known from only one location. Threatened by deforestation and palm oil farming. The species that could solve decaf coffee might disappear before we get to use it properly.

Let's talk caffeine for a moment

Why do coffee plants even produce caffeine?

It's a defence mechanism. Caffeine acts as a natural pest repellent. Insects don't like it. So the plant produces it to protect itself. Smart, right?

For us, caffeine is what delivers that alertness and energy. It also contributes to some of the bitterness we taste in coffee. We talked about measuring caffeine in a previous post.

To give you some numbers, Arabica has about 1.2% caffeine content. Robusta sits higher at around 2.7%. Even decaf coffee, after all that processing, still retains about 0.1%.

Coffea charrieriana? Literally 0%.

Why this plant is different

This is where it gets fascinating.

Coffea charrieriana actually has the genes to produce caffeine. But a mutation in its caffeine synthase gene means it produces theobromine instead. Theobromine is the same compound found in chocolate. The thing that makes chocolate feel good.

This isn't a processing trick. It's genetic. The plant's ancestors diverged from caffeinated coffee species roughly 11 million years ago.

People who have tasted Charrier coffee describe it as mild, lighter in body, almost tea-like with herbaceous notes and gentle acidity. I genuinely doubt if it’s even coffee. It's quite different from the full bodied Arabica we are used to.

The exciting part is what scientists are doing with it. Brazilian developers have already coined the term Decaffito for naturally caffeine-free beans developed from this species. A farm in Costa Rica called Coffea Diversa is experimentally growing it. The real potential though is crossbreeding this with commercial Arabica to create a new generation of naturally low-caffeine coffee. That could be a game changer for the industry.

How decaf used to work

We covered this in our decaf post. Quick recap using the same analogy.

Think of caffeine as mud on a potato. The solvent method uses soap (chemicals) to scrub it off. Quick but leaves some residue. The Swiss Water Process soaks the potato in mud-free water. Clean but takes ages. The CO₂ method is like a fancy pressure washer. Fast and clean but expensive.

All of these happen after the coffee is harvested. They add cost, complexity and some people remain cautious about chemical methods even though they're considered safe.

Coffea charrieriana skips all of that. The bean just grows without caffeine. No processing, no extraction, no chemicals. That's the fundamental difference.

We are still years away from sipping a cup of Charrier coffee at home. The plant is rare, commercially unviable right now and the taste profile needs work. But the fact that nature figured out caffeine-free coffee 11 million years before we started trying with Swiss Water and CO₂ is pretty humbling.

So {{ first_name | friend }}, would you try a naturally caffeine-free coffee if it ever hits the shelves? Or is the caffeine kick too important to give up?


Powered by beehiiv

item.title: Iced Pour Over is better than Cold Brew
item.description: Cold brew gets all the hype. But if you care about tasting your coffee's actual character, an iced pour over might be the better summer drink.
item.dc:creator: Aravind Balla
item.content:encoded:
It's been scorching in Hyderabad lately. Every morning I put the kettle on and immediately regret it the moment the steam hits my face.

Hot coffee season is officially over. Time to talk cold.

People default to cold brew when they think cold black coffee. But there's another way that I think is better. If you already know the difference between a cold brew and an iced pour over, you know your stuff. If not, here's the breakdown.

Cold Brew vs Iced Pour Over

Cold Brew Prep

Cold brew is coffee steeped in cold or room temperature water for 12-24 hours. No heat involved. Time does all the work. The result is smooth, mellow, low on acidity and heavy on body. Think chocolatey, nutty, easy to drink. We covered how to make one in the cold brew tonic post.

Iced pour over, also called Japanese iced coffee or flash brew, is different. You brew a regular hot pour over but directly onto ice sitting in your server. Hot water hits the coffee, extracts everything, drips down and gets chilled instantly by the ice. The cup is bright, fruity, aromatic and crisp.

Same beans. Completely different cups.

Why I Think Iced Pour Overs Are Better

It comes down to extraction.

Iced Pour Over

We talked about how different stages of brewing extract different compounds. First come the acids, the bright citrusy notes. Then the sweetness. And lastly the bitter compounds that add body but can overpower everything else.

Hot water does a proper job of pulling out those acids and sugars. Cold water simply can't dissolve a lot of those delicate compounds. That's why cold brew ends up smooth but flat. The interesting stuff gets left behind.

Clever part is coming up…

In an iced pour over, you're using roughly 60% of your total water as hot water. The rest is ice. So the brew finishes quicker. You're extracting through the sweet spot, the acids and the sugars, and mostly stopping before the bitter compounds have a chance to come out.

The extraction stages are literally working in your favour.

You get brightness and sweetness without the bitterness. The best parts of hot coffee, served cold.

Why Ice In The Server Matters

This is not just about cooling the coffee down. It's about locking flavour in.

When hot coffee drips onto ice, it flash-chills. That rapid cooling traps the volatile aromatic compounds that would otherwise escape into the air. You know that lovely smell that fills the room while you brew? That's flavour leaving your cup.

If you read the extract chilling post, it's a similar idea. That cold steel ball locks in up to 40% more volatiles. The ice in your server does the same thing, just simpler.

Oxidation slows down too. Hot coffee left to cool on its own goes stale. Instant chilling prevents that from happening.

It's Quick

Cold brew needs planning. You have to decide the night before. Steep for 12-24 hours. That's commitment.

Iced pour over? Same time as your regular pour over. 3-4 minutes. You wake up, decide you want cold coffee, brew and drink. No overnight decisions.

You don't need extra equipment either. Same V60, same kettle, same filters. Just put ice in your server before you start brewing.

A Quick Recipe

15g of coffee, ground a couple of clicks finer than your usual setting. About 17-18 clicks on the Timemore C2.

Put 100g of ice in your server. Brew with 150g of hot water as you normally would. The ice is part of your total water, so your ratio stays at 1:16.

Once the brew is done, give the server a good swirl and pour over a glass with fresh ice if you like.

That Said

Cold brew has its place. It's great for batch prep, keeps well in the fridge for days, and works beautifully with milk or as a base for drinks like cold brew tonic. If you want something ready to grab every morning without thinking, cold brew wins on convenience.

But if you drink your coffee black and care about tasting what the beans actually have to offer, iced pour over is the way to go this summer.

That’s all I have. Have a caffeinated weekend.

See you.

Keep on brewing!


Powered by beehiiv

item.title: Comparing 3 common espresso drinks
item.description: Ristretto, macchiato, espresso - three Italian names, three different drinks. Here's what actually sets them apart and when to order which.
item.dc:creator: Aravind Balla
item.content:encoded:
I was at a cafe with Vikra recently. The menu had only ristretto listed. He looked at it and asked, "Why not just name it espresso?"

Fair question. Most people think they're the same thing. Throw macchiato into the mix and it gets even more confusing.

Honestly, I couldn't answer properly then. I only knew that it's 1:1 coffee to water. Now I have post for it, not just for him, but for all of us.

The Names Are the Clue

All three are Italian words. And Italians are very particular about naming their coffee.

Espresso means "pressed out." Hot water is forced through finely ground coffee at 9 bars of pressure in about 25-30 seconds. We talked about this in detail in the Science of an Espresso post. The standard ratio is 1:2, so 18g of coffee in, roughly 36g of liquid out. This is the baseline. The reference point for everything else.

Ristretto means "restricted" or "shortened." Same amount of coffee, but less water and a shorter extraction. About 15-20 seconds instead of 25-30. The ratio drops to around 1:1 or 1:1.5. You get maybe 15-20ml in the cup instead of 30ml. It looks darker, thicker, and more syrupy.

Macchiato means "stained" or "marked." This one has an origin story. In busy Italian cafes, baristas needed a way to tell waiters which cup was a plain espresso and which had a drop of milk in it. So they "marked" the milk one with a dollop of foam on top. That's it. An espresso, stained with milk.

How They Actually Taste

Here's a quick comparison.

Espresso

Ristretto

Macchiato

Ratio

1:2

1:1 to 1:1.5

1:2 + milk

Volume

~30ml

~15-20ml

~40-50ml

Extraction

25-30s

15-20s

25-30s (the shot)

Milk

None

None

Small dollop of foam

Character

Balanced, full spectrum

Sweeter, bolder, less bitter

Espresso-forward, softened edges

Espresso gives you the full picture. Acids, sweetness, bitterness, body. Everything the bean has to offer, extracted in balance. It's the most "complete" of the three.

Ristretto cuts the extraction short. Remember how we talked about extraction happening in stages? Acids and sweetness come first, bitterness comes last. By stopping early, the ristretto skips most of the bitter compounds. What you get is a sweeter, more concentrated shot. Some people consider this the purest form of espresso. Interestingly, it also has slightly less caffeine because of the shorter extraction time.

This actually applies well for pour overs as well. It's something I am experimenting with, where I only do the first 2 or 3 pours and skip the last one. I might have a post later.

Macchiato keeps the full espresso shot intact but softens it with a tiny amount of milk foam. It's for when you find espresso too harsh but a cappuccino too milky. The milk moderates without overwhelming. In our cappuccino post, we placed it on the milk spectrum. Macchiato sits right next to espresso, with the least milk of any milk based drink.

While we are at it, there's a fourth variation worth knowing. Lungo means "long" in Italian. It's the opposite of a ristretto. Same coffee dose but more water, extracted for 45-60 seconds instead of the usual 25-30. You get a larger, milder cup. I talked about this briefly in the Americano & Long Black post. The key difference from an Americano is that all the water in a lungo passes through the coffee. With an Americano, you add water after the shot is pulled. This means a lungo extracts more of those later bitter compounds, so it can taste harsher if not dialled in well. Personally, I don't reach for it often, but it's good to know where it sits in the family.

When Would You Pick Each?

Fun fact: some cafes use ristretto shots as the default in their flat whites. The sweetness pairs better with milk than a standard espresso pull.

Next time you are at a cafe, try ordering all three side by side. Same beans, three different experiences. That's the beauty of espresso based drinks.

So {{ first_name | friend }}, which one are you reaching for? And if you've tried a ristretto before, did you notice the difference? Let me know.

That’s all I have. Have a caffeinated weekend.

See you.

Keep on brewing!


Powered by beehiiv

item.title: Cutting Coffee - Cortado
item.description: Cortado is my favourite milk based coffee drink. Here's what makes it special, where it originated, how it's different from other drinks, and how you can try one at home.
item.dc:creator: Aravind Balla
item.content:encoded:
Cortado is my favourite milk based coffee drink. This is the one that I order at cafes, other than pour overs, of course. It does exactly what it's intended to do. And it looks cute too. That tiny glass with a smooth, warm drink sitting in it.

Let me tell you why I like it and what makes it special.

A Drink That "Cuts"

The word "cortado" comes from the Spanish verb cortar, which means "to cut." The milk cuts the espresso's acidity and intensity. That's literally all it is.

It originated in Spain's Basque region in the early 20th century. It was a working-class drink. Quick, affordable, meant for morning breaks. Workers would grab one, sip it, and get back to work.

From Spain, it travelled to Latin America during colonization. Cuba adapted it into something called the cortadito, where they sweetened it with condensed milk because fresh milk was hard to come by. This makes sense given Cuba's sugarcane culture. Everything is sweeter there.

In the US, it became popular through Blue Bottle Coffee in San Francisco. They served it in a small Libbey glass called the "Gibraltar." So if you see a "Gibraltar" on the menu at a specialty cafe, that's essentially a cortado.

What goes into a Cortado

The recipe is dead simple. Equal parts espresso and steamed milk. Usually a double shot (around 60ml) with the same amount of steamed milk. The total drink is about 120ml.

The milk is steamed but not frothed. There is no foam cap, no thick microfoam. Just warm, silky milk mixed right into the espresso. The drink is uniform. One colour, one texture.

It is served in a small glass, usually without a handle. It's meant to be sipped and finished, not nursed for an hour.

To draw parallels, the espresso is the star here and milk is just the supporting act that takes the edge off.

How is it different

Here's a simple comparison.

Drink

Espresso

Milk

Foam

Size

Macchiato

dominant

a splash

a dot

~60ml

Cortado

1/2

1/2

none

~120ml

Flat White

1/3

2/3

micro-thin

~160ml

Cappuccino

1/3

1/3

1/3 thick

~180ml

A macchiato is espresso with just a "mark" of milk. Cortado goes further with equal parts. A flat white is bigger and more milk forward. Cappuccino has that thick foam dome on top.

Think of cortado as the middle ground between a macchiato and a flat white. Espresso forward, but smooth enough to not punch you in the face.

When I Reach For One

When I want espresso but need the edge taken off. Just something small and satisfying.

It's also a great way to judge a cafe's espresso. The milk softens it just enough without hiding anything. If the espresso is bad, you'll know.

At home, I sometimes use the mokapot as a base. Equal parts mokapot coffee and warm milk. It's not the same as a proper cortado but gets you close enough. The most important thing, texture, will be missing.

So {{ first_name | friend }}, have you tried a cortado? I wonder what your favourite milk drink is.

That’s all I have. Have a caffeinated weekend.

See you.

Keep on brewing!


Powered by beehiiv

item.title: Our Experience at the International Coffee Festival, Bengaluru
item.description: We attended the IICF this year and this is the reason we were over-caffeinated.
item.dc:creator: Aravind Balla
item.content:encoded:
Vikra and I attended the India International Coffee Festival (IICF) in Bangalore last week. It was a 3-day event and we went for the last two days.

We left with shaky hands, racing hearts, and way too many ideas.

Turns out, saying "yes" to every sample at a coffee festival has consequences. But I'd do it again. Every single sip of it.

The championships were unreal

If you've never watched a barista championship live, it's hard to explain how intense it gets. These aren't people casually making lattes. They're performing under a clock, in front of judges, with techniques they've rehearsed for months.

The stories they come up with are the best part.

The brewing championship was my favourite. Watching someone dial in a recipe with that level of precision, adjusting grind size by microns, timing pours to the second. It makes you realise how much depth there is to a "simple" cup of coffee.

And then there were the cocktail rounds.

One recipe I immediately wrote down: an Irish coffee made with a pour over. The barista brewed a clean pour over, added Jameson whiskey, and floated sweet cream on top. Not your typical Irish coffee with a French press or drip. A pour over. I'm definitely trying this at home. Wish me luck. Wish my kitchen luck.

At some point I also took over brewing at the Project Kaapi stall. They're a roaster from Hyderabad, like us. It was fun being on the other side, making pour overs for people walking by.

Osmotic flow and the Cafec founder

This was the highlight I didn't expect.

The founder of Cafec gave a session on osmotic flow. He explained the science behind how water moves through coffee grounds and why it matters for extraction. He also explained why you don't need to pre-wet Cafec's Abaca filters, which goes against what most of us have been taught.

Here's what made it special. He spoke entirely in Japanese. There was a translator for us, but honestly, you didn't always need one. The way he talked about his work, the gestures, the energy, the pride. It transcended language.

There's something beautiful about watching someone explain their life's work with that kind of passion. You could tell this wasn't a sales pitch. This was a person who genuinely loves the science of brewing and has spent decades thinking about how water and paper and coffee interact.

I walked out of that session looking at the Cafec dripper very differently.

We met some of you

We sent out an email the day before saying we'll be at the festival. A couple of you showed up and said hi. It was nice to put faces to the people reading these emails.

Between meeting readers and even reading all the replies we get, it makes the whole thing feel real. Thanks for coming out.

The festival hangover

I don't mean alcohol, though those championship cocktail recipes are tempting.

I mean the kind where your brain is buzzing with too many ideas. New brewing methods to try, recipes to recreate, people to follow up with, and a newfound respect for filter paper science.

Also, my resting heart rate was noticeably higher for about two days after. Worth it.

If there is anything you would like to know more, let me know. And if have been there, I'd love to hear about your experience.

That’s all I have. Have a caffeinated weekend.

See you.

Keep on brewing!


Powered by beehiiv

item.title: Cappuccino - The Drink That Wears a Hood
item.description: The coffee drink which is, surprisingly, not from Italy.
item.dc:creator: Aravind Balla
item.content:encoded:
I was at a cafe the other day, staring at the menu and a friend asked me, "what's actually the difference between cappuccino, latte and flat white?"

I opened my mouth. Paused. And realized I didn't have a clean answer.

So I went down the rabbit hole. And it turns out, the cappuccino has one of the most interesting origin stories in the coffee world.

A Drink Named After Monks

The word "cappuccino" literally means "little hood" in Italian. It comes from the Capuchin monks: a Catholic order known for their simple brown hooded robes. When you pour milk into espresso and that rich brown color appears, it looks exactly like the color of those robes.

But the cappuccino we know today didn't start in Italy.

It started as the Kapuziner in 1700s Vienna which is coffee mixed with cream and sugar, sometimes spices. A very different drink.

Kapuziner from Vienna

It wasn't until espresso machines took over Italy post-WW2 that the modern cappuccino was born. Espresso pulled through pressure, topped with steamed milk and a thick cap of foam.

Fun fact - in Italy, it became a strictly morning drink. Order a cappuccino after 11am there, and you've just outed yourself as a tourist.

What Actually Goes Into One

The classic cappuccino follows a simple ratio: 1/3 espresso, 1/3 steamed milk, 1/3 microfoam.

That's it. But the magic is in the foam.

Surprised there is no art? More on it below.

A good cappuccino has thick, velvety foam that holds its shape, not the bubbly, airy kind you get from a French press frother. The milk is steamed to around 60-65°C, which is the sweet spot where lactose breaks down and the milk tastes naturally sweet.

It's a drink about texture as much as taste. That pillowy foam layer on top, the warm milk underneath, and a strong espresso punch cutting through it all.

So How Is It Different From a Latte or Flat White?

This is the question that started this whole post. Here's the simplest way to see it:

Drink

Espresso

Steamed Milk

Foam

Cappuccino

1/3

1/3

1/3 (thick)

Latte

1/6

4/6

1/6 (thin)

Flat White

1/3

2/3

micro-thin layer

Cappuccino has the strongest coffee flavor of the three. You taste the espresso through the foam.

Latte is the milkiest where coffee takes a backseat. It's smooth, gentle, and the go-to order for people who want coffee-flavored milk. No shade if you prefer this.

Flat White sits in between. It's espresso-forward like a cappuccino but silky smooth like a latte which no foam cap sitting on top. Just a thin microfoam layer blended into the milk.

But What About the Art?

Latte art was born on, you guessed it, lattes. Thinner foam gives you a flat canvas, making it easy to pour hearts and rosettas. With traditional cappuccinos, you'd just get a white dome of foam. No canvas, no art.

But third-wave cafes (not to be confused with Third Wave Coffee) changed the game. Baristas started texturing cappuccino foam thinner, closer to flat white consistency, so they could pour art on top. Walk into any specialty cafe today and your cappuccino probably has a rosetta on it.

There's a tradeoff though. A cappuccino with latte art has less foam than a traditional one. Purists will tell you that's basically just a small latte. They're not entirely wrong. But honestly, if it looks beautiful and tastes great , does the label really matter?

So, are you a cappuccino person, a latte person, or a flat white person?

That’s all I have. Have a caffeinated weekend.

See you.

Keep brewing!


Powered by beehiiv

item.title: SCA Sensory Skills Certification: What I Learned and Unlearned
item.description: I went to the coffee school and got certified. Here's what the training was like, and whether it's worth it for home brewers.
item.dc:creator: Aravind Balla
item.content:encoded:
After 4+ years of brewing speciality coffee at home, reading about it, writing about it — I finally got officially certified. SCA Sensory Skills, Foundation and Intermediate. Here's what that was like.

Am I tasting just water?

Why get certified?

You can learn a lot on your own. YouTube, blogs, trial and error — I've done all of that. But there was always this itch. Am I tasting this right? Is my understanding of acidity actually correct? After years of pushing it off, I finally committed.

SCA stands for the Speciality Coffee Association — the global body behind coffee standards, grading, and education. Their Sensory Skills module specifically teaches you how to evaluate coffee. Not brew it. Evaluate it. It's about tasting with intention.

The training: SCAI, Bangalore

I trained at the Speciality Coffee Association of India (SCAI) in Bangalore. 4-day course — 2 days for Foundation, 2 days for Intermediate. I initially signed up only for the Foundation. But by the end of day two, I knew I had to continue. The Foundation was going so well that skipping the Intermediate felt like leaving mid-conversation.

Mornings were theory. Afternoons were all cupping — tables full of cups, lots of slurping, and discussions about what everyone was tasting. The group was a mix of roasters, café owners, and enthusiasts like me.

What I unlearned

This was the best part. Honestly, the unlearning was more valuable than the learning.

Acidity ≠ sour. I had a skewed understanding of acidity in coffee. For years, I associated high acidity with something unpleasant — something to avoid. Turns out, acidity is brightness. Liveliness. It's a quality attribute. When done right, it's what makes a cup interesting. I was rating coffees down for the very thing that made them good.

Flavour notes and taste qualities are independent. This one was a revelation. If a coffee bag says "orange", or you identify it as a flavour note, it doesn't mean the coffee should be highly acidic. Flavour descriptors and qualities like acidity, sweetness, body — they exist on independent axes. You can have an orange-noted coffee that's low in acidity. I'd been conflating the two for years.

What makes coffee "good" isn't randomness. I thought I had a decent sense for quality. Well, I was partially right. The SCA framework gives you a structured way to score coffee across specific attributes — fragrance, acidity, body, balance, aftertaste, and more. It recalibrated my palate. What I used to call "nice" now has specific words and scores attached to it.

Realizing you've been confidently wrong about some things is really humbling. Four years of brewing doesn't make you immune to bad assumptions.

What I learned

One of the first activities in the Foundation was tasting the basic tastes — sweet, sour, bitter, salt, and umami — in water. Then we mapped where on our tongues we felt each taste. Turns out, it can be slightly different for each person.

And yes, umami is found in coffee. It's hard to describe — it's that feeling of enjoying something and also not being fully satiated. If you've had Maggi or anything with MSG, you know the taste. Finding it in coffee was unexpected.

Oh boy, I took a lot of notes.

The cupping protocol changed how I evaluate new coffee bags at home. There's a specific ratio, water temperature, steep time, and a method for breaking the crust. It's simple once you learn it, but it gives you a much cleaner read on a coffee than just brewing it your usual way.

The biggest shift was learning to isolate specific taste attributes instead of going with a general "this is nice". You learn to separate acidity from sweetness from body, score each independently, and build a more complete picture.

And triangulation tests — where you identify the odd cup out from three — are harder than you'd think. I tried this experiment with my coffee-drinking friends after the course, and they loved it. Great party trick if your friends are into coffee.

Was it worth it?

Passed both levels and it is genuinely satisfying.

Would I recommend it to a home brewer? Yes — especially if you've been at it for a while and feel like you've plateaued. It won't make your coffee taste better. But it'll make you taste better.

If you're even slightly curious, go for it. The unlearning alone is worth the experience.

That’s all I have. Have a caffeinated weekend.

See you.

Keep on brewing!


Powered by beehiiv

item.title: Pour Over Extraction
item.description: How to extract for different notes, brightness, body, and acidity
item.dc:creator: Aravind Balla
item.content:encoded:
"How do I get the blueberry out of this Ethiopian?"

I see this question pop up constantly. Someone buys a bag with tasting notes promising blueberry, jasmine, and citrus. They brew it. They taste... coffee. Maybe some sourness. Maybe some bitterness. But blueberry? Where is it hiding?

Here's the uncomfortable truth: you might never taste exactly what the roaster tasted. And that's okay. But there are things you can do to get closer.

Why Tasting Notes Are Slippery

Before we dive into extraction, let's set expectations.

Those tasting notes on the bag were written by a cupper who tasted the coffee under specific conditions – usually as a cupping, not a pour over. They might have used different water, different equipment, and they've calibrated their palate over thousands of cups.

You're not wrong if you taste something different. You're just not calibrated to their team.

That said, the flavours are real. They exist in the bean. The question is how to coax them out.

The Extraction EQ

Think of extraction like a three-band equalizer.

High notes – acids, brightness, tartness. These extract first and fastest, even at lower temperatures. The sharp citrus zing, the tangy berry notes.

Mid notes – sweetness, fruit character, floral aromatics. This is where the "blueberry" and "jasmine" live. The compounds that make each coffee distinct.

Low notes – body, bitterness, roasty depth. These extract last and need more persuasion.

A balanced cup has all three in harmony. But if you're chasing a specific note, you need to understand which band it lives in and how to bring it forward.

Practical Adjustments

Here's where it gets actionable.

For More Fruit and Floral Notes (Mid-Range)

These are the hardest to extract consistently. They need enough extraction to come out, but not so much that they get buried by bitterness.

Extend your bloom. Instead of the standard 30-45 seconds, try 60-90 seconds. Longer bloom times push the mid notes forward. I've had success with 2-minute blooms on stubborn light roasts using a coarser grind.

Increase agitation during bloom. Give it a good stir, or swirl the brewer. This helps saturate all grounds evenly and pulls more of those delicate aromatics.

Use hotter water. For light roasts, push to 96°C or even just off boil. Those dense beans need heat to open up. One experiment found that 211°F (99°C) throughout the brew produced the fruitiest results compared to dropping temperature mid-brew.

Try a higher ratio. 1:16.5 or 1:17 can increase fruit and acidity perception. You're extracting more from the same amount of coffee.

For More Brightness and Acidity (High Notes)

If you want that citrus zing or berry tartness to pop:

Coarser grind, tighter ratio. A 1:14 or 1:15 ratio with a coarser grind produces a sweet cup with pronounced acidity and lighter body.

Faster brew time. Aim for 2:30-2:45 total. Less contact time means more acids, less bitterness.

Let it cool. Seriously. Many fruit notes become more apparent as the coffee drops to 60-70°C. Take your time sipping.

For More Sweetness and Body (Balancing the Low End)

Sometimes the fruit notes are there but feel thin or disconnected:

Finer grind, slower extraction. More contact time brings out sugar browning flavours and body.

Multiple pours with gentle agitation. This builds body without over-extracting.

Rest your beans longer. Fresh-off-roast coffee can taste one-dimensional. Day 7-12 often shows the best balance for light roasts.

The Freshness Factor

This one surprised me when I first learned it.

Floral and fruity notes are volatile – they're the first to fade as beans age. Some cuppers report peak florals straight out of the roaster, fading by day 3-4. Others find day 5-12 is the sweet spot.

There's no universal answer here. But if you bought beans specifically for their fruity notes and they taste flat, check the roast date. If it's been 3+ weeks, the blueberry might have already left the building.

What If You Still Can't Taste It?

A few honest possibilities.

The beans might just not be that fruity. Tasting notes vary lot to lot. A roaster might have cupped a different batch than what ended up in your bag.

Your palate needs calibration. While eating a blueberry, sense how it tastes. Your brain will start making connections.

Your water might be off. TDS between 40-140 ppm with good mineral balance makes a huge difference. Hard water or distilled water both flatten flavour.

Your grinder produces too many fines. Excessive fines cause uneven extraction, muddying the cup. Sifting with a tea strainer can help if you suspect this.

The honest answer to "how do I get blueberry from this Ethiopian" is: maybe you will, maybe you won't. But pushing your extraction with higher temps, longer blooms, and proper agitation gives you the best shot.

And sometimes the coffee just tastes like really good coffee. That's enough too.

So {{ first_name | friend }}, what tasting note have you been chasing? Have you managed to nail it, or is it still elusive?

See you and have a caffeinated weekend.

Keep brewing!
